sections.heading: Definition and Anatomy
sections.content: Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma (SBA) is a primary malignant neoplasm arising from the glandular epithelium of the small intestine. The small intestine is anatomically divided into the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum, which originate embryologically from the midgut (except for the first portion of the duodenum, which derives from the foregut).

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.content: SBA is a relatively rare malignancy, accounting for approximately 30% to 40% of all small intestinal cancers, and comprises only about 3% of gastrointestinal tract cancers [1, 2]. The median age at diagnosis is 66 years, with a roughly equal sex distribution [2].

sections.heading: Anatomic Distribution
sections.content: While the majority of SBA cases arise in the duodenum (52%–58%), the remainder develop in the jejunum (15%–29%), ileum (10%–13%), or an unspecified location (4%–16%) [3, 4].

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: The disease often presents at a more advanced stage and higher grade compared to colorectal cancer (CRC), and common clinical presentations include complications such as gastric outlet obstruction (for duodenal primaries) or cramping abdominal pain (for jejunal/ileal primaries) [3, 5].

sections.heading: Prognosis and Treatment
sections.content: Due to its rarity and often advanced presentation at diagnosis, SBA carries a generally poor prognosis, with a 5-year relative survival rate of 42% for distant metastatic disease compared to 85% for localized disease [6]. The cornerstone of curative-intent treatment for localized disease is surgical resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes [7].
